coredata.dc:title: Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene oxide quantum dots by photochemical fluorination
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Fuhua
Liu, Fuchi
Bai, Xiaohua
Xu, Xiaofen
Kong, Wenjie
Liu, Jun
Lv, Fengzhen
Long, Lizhen
Yang, Yong
Li, Ming
coredata.dc:description: Tailoring the band gap and understanding the underlying mechanism are important for extending the applications of graphene quantum dots in optoelectronics. In this work, we synthesize fluorinated graphene oxide quantum dots (F-GOQDs) through a photochemical method, and study their optical properties. The obtained F-GOQDs exhibit a maximal blue-shift of photoluminescence (PL) emission of ca. 77 nm compared to that of the graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). The PL shift results from the substitution of the hydroxyls and carbonyls groups with fluorine during the fluorination process, which can tune the band gap of the GOQDs. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations support our proposed mechanism for band gap tuning in the GOQDs through the use of fluorination.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11664003 , 11474285 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2015GXNSFAA139015 ), and the Scientific Research and Technology Development Program of Guilin (No. 2016012002 ), and the Key Project of Guangxi Normal University (No. 2014ZD003 ), P. R. China. We gratefully acknowledge the crew of Center for Computational Materials Science of the Institute for Materials Research, Tohoku University for their continuous support of the SR16000 supercomputing facilities. We thank the staff of the Hefei Branch of Supercomputing Center of Chinese Academy of Sciences for their support of supercomputing resources. Fuhua Gao and Fuchi Liu contributed equally to this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the home-built experiment system. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Typical TEM images of (a) GOQDs, (b) F-GOQDs-3. Inset of (a) and (b): HR-TEM images of GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3. Size distributions of (c) GOQDs, (d) F-GOQDs-3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
(a) The survey XPS spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs. (b) The typical C1s XPS of GOQDs and F-GOQDs. (c) The Raman spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra for GOQDs and F-GOQDs. (b) Optical photographs of GOQDs and F-GOQDs dispersed in water being excited by a UV beam of 365 nm. (c–f) PL spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs at different excitation wavelengths. (g) Normalized PL spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs excited at 333 nm. (h–i) PLE spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Calculated optical absorption spectra of GOQD (solid line) and F-GOQD (dashed line). (a) OH and F GOQD; (b) CO and F GOQD. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Calculated optical absorption spectra of GOQD (solid line) and F-GOQD (dashed line), based on a larger-sized GQD (inset). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
The contents of chemical groups of GOQDs and F-GOQDs derived from the quantitative analysis of the XPS.

head.ce:title: Tuning the photoluminescence of graphene oxide quantum dots by photochemical fluorination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Technology, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Physics and Technology, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin, 541004, China.
Corresponding author. Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tailoring the band gap and understanding the underlying mechanism are important for extending the applications of graphene quantum dots in optoelectronics. In this work, we synthesize fluorinated graphene oxide quantum dots (F-GOQDs) through a photochemical method, and study their optical properties. The obtained F-GOQDs exhibit a maximal blue-shift of photoluminescence (PL) emission of ca. 77 nm compared to that of the graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs). The PL shift results from the substitution of the hydroxyls and carbonyls groups with fluorine during the fluorination process, which can tune the band gap of the GOQDs. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations support our proposed mechanism for band gap tuning in the GOQDs through the use of fluorination.

ce:section.ce:para: Over the past few years, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have received increasing attention because of their unique electronic and opto-electronic properties and a wide range of potential applications [1–9]. GQDs have a band gap due to quantum confinement effect [7,10], and exhibit strong photoluminescence (PL) [1,3]. Some studies have demonstrated the possibility of tailoring the band gap or PL emission of GQDs by chemical functionalization of GQDs.

It has been demonstrated that functionalizing the GQDs with fluorine element can produce new phenomena and provide a means to extend their application [11–19]. For example, Gong et al. [17] reported that water-soluble and blue luminescent graphene fluoroxide quantum dots (GFOQDs) with tunable fluorine coverage and size were effectively synthesized from exfoliated fluorinated graphene (FG). The GFOQDs exhibited resist pH effects and displayed stable luminescence in both acid and alkali conditions. Feng et al. [19] synthesized fluorinated graphene quantum dots (F-GQDs) by cutting the FG through hydrothermal method, and demonstrated that the PL emission from the F-GQDs showed a clear red-shift of ca. 10 nm compared to that of the GQDs [20]. They claimed that the relatively high surface defect concentration in the F-GQDs may lead to the red-shifted emission. Luo et al. [15] prepared the F-GQDs by mixing GQDs and XeF2 in a facile gaseous phase heating method, and found that the PL emission of the F-GQDs exhibited an obvious blue-shift of 90 nm compared to that of the GQDs. The observed PL blue-shift may be due to the relatively strong electron affinity of F atoms in the F-GQDs. Overall, the fluorination is an effective way for tuning the band gap or PL emission of GQDs. However, the chemically inert bonds and hydrophobic nature of FG prevent it from being easily cut into quantum dots [17,19,21]. This further makes it impossible to simply transplant the methods that are previously employed to prepare GQDs into F-GQDs. So far, only a few methods have been reported to produce F-GQDs [11,13–19,22]. In addition, the mechanism for the band gap tuning or PL shifting of the GQDs through fluorination is far from clear and requires further study. Therefore, no matter from the perspective of laboratory investigations or for the sake of its further technological applications, an effective and facile method to prepare F-GQDs is highly desired [17].

In this work, we demonstrated a simple and feasible way to synthesize the fluorinated graphene oxide quantum dots (F-GOQDs) by irradiating the mixtures of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs) and XeF2 through a photochemical method. The PL emission of the F-GOQDs exhibits a maximal blue-shift of about 77 nm compared to that of the GQDs. The mechanism for the change in the PL emission of the GOQDs by fluorination is supported by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. Based on the experimental and computational analysis, it is found that hydroxyls and carbonyls are replaced by fluorine atoms during the photochemical fluorination process, which lead to the PL blue-shift of the F-GOQDs. To our knowledge, this is the first study that tunes and identifies the change of the band gap in GOQDs by fluorination using a photochemical method.

GOQDs were synthesized by our previously reported method [23,24]. As illustrated in Fig. 1 , the F-GOQDs were prepared by a modified method for synthesizing of nitrogen-doped GQDs through a photochemical route [24]. The mass ratio of XeF2 to GOQDs was adjusted to obtain samples with different degree of fluorination. The corresponding samples were denoted as F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3 with a mass ratio of XeF2 to GOQDs of 5:1, 10:1, and 25:1, respectively. Briefly, the mixtures of GOQDs and XeF2 were put in a quartz boat, and then were placed inside the quartz tube which has two gas valves in the gas inlet (named as valve A) and the gas outlet (named as valve B), respectively. Then, the quartz tube was located in two brackets, after that, the air in the quartz tube was excluded by importing argon (purity ∼99.999%) for 10 min. Then the valve B and valve A were closed successively; after that, the mixtures of GOQDs and XeF2 were irradiated for 10 min with light from a 500 W high-pressure Hg lamp in the pressure-tight quartz tube. After cooling the quartz tube to room temperature, the F-GOQDs were obtained.

The morphologies of the samples were detected by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 F) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) using Al Kα radiation. The PL spectra were measured at ambient conditions by a spectrofluorophotometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) using a Xe lamp as the light source.

The first-principles calculations were carried out using the VASP code [25,26], which is based on density functional theory (DFT). The electron wave functions were expanded using a plane wave basis set, and the electron-ion interactions were treated by the PAW potentials [27,28]. The exchange-correlation interactions of electrons are described by the PBE type functional [29]. The energy cutoff for plane waves is 600 eV. For the total energy calculations of graphene quantum dot (GQD) systems, an 8 × 8 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack k-mesh [30] is generated for sampling the Brillouin zone (BZ).

The GQDs are modeled by two different-sized systems: the C atoms around the geometric center of a (5 × 5) and (9 × 9) supercell of graphene sheet (hexagonal cell with the dimensional lengths of ∼12.3 Å × 12.3 Å, ∼22.14 Å × 22.14 Å, respectively), in which the marginal atoms in the supercell are removed. Each GQD is separated by a vacuum layer of ∼15 Å in the surface normal to minimize the artificial interactions due to periodic boundary condition. The graphene oxide quantum dot (GOQD) is simulated by decorating OH (or O) groups at the edge sites of the GQD, and the effects of fluorination are studied by partly replacing OH (or O) with F.

Fig. 2 a and b shows the typical TEM images of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3. These figures reveal that the GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3 are relatively uniformly distributed without agglomeration. The insets of Fig. 2a and b shows the high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3, which indicate that both of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3 have high crystallinity, and the lattice spacing of GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3 is 0.21 nm, and 0.22 nm, respectively. The size distributions of GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3 are shown in Fig. 2c and d, respectively. It is found that both the GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3 have the diameters of ca. 2–6 nm. According to the statistical calculations, the average sizes of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs-3 are approximately 3.5 nm and 3.7 nm, respectively. These results demonstrate that the functionalization of the GOQDs with fluorine does not significantly alter their microstructures.

To detect the fluorine content and the bonding environment of the C and F species of the samples of F-GOQDs, XPS was employed. Fig. 3 a gives the survey XPS spectra of the samples. It is found that F-GOQDs display a prominent peak at 686 eV, which corresponds to photoemission of the F 1s core level [31]. This result indicates that the fluorine element has been successfully introduced into GOQDs. With the increase of the mass ratio of XeF2 to GOQDs, one can see that (i) the intensity of the F 1s signal of F-GOQDs increases gradually, (ii) by comparing to that of GOQDs, the O 1s signal of F-GOQDs at ca. 532 eV decreases gradually. As shown in Table 1 , the GOQDs, F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3 have high oxygen contents of 30.18 at %, 27.84 at %, 25.62 at %, and 9.99 at % respectively, implying that they are oxygen-rich. The presence of oxygen-rich functional groups can provide F-GOQDs with excellent water solubility for practical applications. One can find that the F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3 have a high F content of 16.06 at %, 28.07 at %, and 53.17 at %, respectively. This suggests that GOQDs were fluorinated with high-content F after irradiation only for 10 min, and the F content of the F-GOQDs can be tuned by adjusting the mass ratio of XeF2 to GOQDs.

Fig. 3b shows the typical C1s XPS spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs, which were fine-scanned and deconvoluted into several subpeaks. The intense peak at 284.5 eV corresponds to the CC sp2 bonds, while the peak at 285.5 eV is contributed by CC bonding. It suggests that the sp2, and sp3 hybridization carbon atoms co-exist in both GOQDs and F-GOQDs. Compared to the GOQDs, the F-GOQDs show an obvious increase in the content of sp3 CC bonds, along with the decreasing of the sp2 CC bonds. Furthermore, several peaks center at 285.3, 286.7, and 288.7 eV, which are attributed to the COH (hydroxyl), COC (epoxy) and CO (carbonyl) bonding, respectively, as evidenced in C 1s XPS spectrum of both GOQDs and F-GOQDs. The peaks centered at about 289.2, 289.9, 291.6, and 293.4 eV are ascribed to weak CF (w-CF), covalent CF, CF2, and CF3 bonds [17,32], respectively.

On the basis of the XPS C 1s spectra, the atomic ratios of the different oxygenic groups for GOQDs and F-GOQDs were calculated and shown in Table 1. It is found that the hydroxyl (COH) content decreases from 8.10 at % for GOQDs to 6.13 at %, 5.66 at %, and 0 for F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3, respectively. In addition, the carbonyl (CO) content obviously decreases from 15.35 at % for GOQDs to 11.05 at %, 8.74 at %, and 3.95 at % for F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3, respectively. It was demonstrated that oxygenic groups in graphene sheets promoted the process of fluorination and facilitated the formation of covalent CF bonds, and the hydroxyls and carbonyls can be easily substituted by fluorine radical to form CF bonds [31]. In view of the fact that the F-GOQDs have high contents of CF bonds along with the obvious decrease of hydroxyl and carbonyl (Table 1), one naturally sees that the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups in GOQDs can promote the reaction of fluorination, and then the F-GOQDs with high contents of fluorine can be obtained.

It is worth noting that the relative content of O/C in F-GOQDs-1 and F-GOQDs-2 becomes higher than in GOQDs. However, it becomes lower in F-GOQDs-3 than in GOQDs. It has been demonstrated that the epoxy functional group shows higher thermal stability than that of hydroxyl and carboxylic acids [33]. This may be one of the reasons that there was no significant reduction of the content of COC bond in the F-GOQDs. Note that water molecule will be formed during the decomposing of the oxygenic groups during the fluorination of the GOQDs, which will lead to the generation of oxygen from the reaction of water molecule with F2. It is reasonable to speculate that the generating oxygen molecules may be split into highly reactive oxygen radicals similarly to the photochemical chlorination of graphene [34], which combine with GOQDs and form new COC bond by a free radical addition reaction and result in the increase of the content of COC in the F-GOQDs-1 and F-GOQDs-2 (Table 1). Notably, compared to that of F-GOQDs-1 and F-GOQDs-2, there is a non-negligible formation of CF3 bond (terminal CF3: 3.25%) in the F-GOQDs-3, which generally located at graphene defects and edges [31,32,35]. Considering the fact that the F content of the F-GOQDs-3 is high up to 53.17 at% (F/C = 1.44), one can speculate that some CC bonds in the GOQDs may be ruptured during the violent fluorination of the GOQDs with excess of XeF2 under the irradiation. It will lead to more defects and edges and facilitate the formation of more CF3 bond in the F-GOQDs-3. This may be the reason why the F-GOQDs-3 has lower content of COC bond than that of F-GOQDs-1 and F-GOQDs-2. However, the intricate reactive mechanism of the fluorination of GOQDs is still unclear; it still needs to be further studied.

Fig. 3c displays the Raman spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs. It is known that the D band is a disordered band which arises from the disorder in the sp2 hybridized carbon, while the G band corresponds to the first-order scattering of the stretching vibration mode E2 g observed for the sp2 carbon domains [36]. One can see that both D peaks of GOQDs and F-GOQDs center at 1400 cm−1. However, the G peak of F-GOQDs exhibits a blue-shift of ca. 17 cm−1 in contrast to that of GOQDs, the shift of the G band may be due to the structural distortion of the F-GOQDs caused by the different bond distances of CC and CF. Furthermore, the calculated relative D-band to G-band intensity ratio (ID/IG) is 0.61, 0.63, and 0.74 for F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3, respectively, larger than that of GOQDs (0.56), which indicates that the F-GOQDs possess a higher disorder of arrangement than GOQDs.

Based on the experimental results presented here, one sees that the photochemical method is an effective way for synthesizing F-GOQDs and tuning their surface chemistry structure of GOQDs. Furthermore, tuning the band gap or PL emission is one of the most important goals to synthesize F-GOQDs. Therefore, investigations on the optical performance of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs have been carefully conducted. Fig. 4 a shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs. One can see that both the GOQDs and F-GOQDs show an absorption band at ca. 225 nm and another absorption band at ca. 300 nm. Meanwhile, Fig. 4b shows the aqueous solutions of GOQDs and F-GOQDs under irradiation by a 365 nm lamp. One can clearly find that the solution of GOQDs, F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3 emits bright yellow, green yellow, wathet blue, and blue luminescence, respectively. Briefly, functionalizing the GOQDs with fluorine can significantly change the optical property of GOQDs.

The difference of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs in the emission color indicates that they might display different PL performance. To have a better understanding of the PL properties of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs, the PL and photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum measurements were carried out. It is known that fluorescent carbon materials commonly exhibit excitation-dependent PL behavior [17,37,38]. Fig. 4c to f shows the PL behaviors of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs. It is found that the GOQDs exhibit an excitation-independent PL behavior with an emission peak at 525 nm when the excitation wavelengths changed from 280 nm to 460 nm (Fig. 4c). However, for the same excitation wavelengths which vary from 280 nm to 460 nm, the F-GOQDs show a different PL behavior. One can see that the F-GOQDs-1 shows an emission peak at ca. 503 nm and has an excitation-dependent PL behavior when the excitation wavelengths changed from 280 nm to 460 nm (Fig. 4d). When the excitation wavelengths changed from 280 nm to 360 nm, the F-GOQDs-2 and F-GOQDs-3 have an excitation-independent PL behavior with an emission peak at 453 nm, and 448 nm, respectively. However, the F-GOQDs-2 and F-GOQDs-3 have an excitation-dependent PL behavior when the excitation wavelengths varied from 380 nm to 460 nm. Specifically, one can see that a second PL peak at ca. 525 nm at the excitation wavelength of 460 nm is also observed in F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3, similar to the PL behavior of the original GOQDs, which implies that a small portion of GOQDs is still available in the F-GOQDs. Fig. 4g shows the normalized PL intensity of the GOQDs and F-GOQDs with an excitation wavelength of 333 nm. One can clearly see that the PL emission peaks of the GOQDs, F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3 locate at 525 nm, 503 nm, 453 nm and 448 nm, respectively. In other words, the PL emission of the F-GOQDs-3 exhibits a maximal blue-shift of 77 nm compares to that of GOQDs. Fig. 3h and i presents the PLE spectra of GOQDs and F-GOQDs. It is seen that the PLE spectrum of the GOQDs is much different from that of the F-GOQDs. The GOQDs show two strong peaks at 333 nm and 466 nm under the monitor of 525 nm. However, both of the F-GOQDs-1, F-GOQDs-2, and F-GOQDs-3 show a strong peak at 333 nm under the monitor of 503 nm, 453 nm, and 448 nm, respectively.

It is known that a reduction reaction always accompanies the functionalization process under the irradiation. Thus, to approximately clarify the role of oxygen functional groups in the PL blue-shift of the F-GOQDs, the PL spectra of the reduced GOQDs (rGOQDs) obtained by irradiating the GOQDs only in Argon for 10 min is also needed for measuring. It was demonstrated that the obtained rGOQDs had lower oxygen content than GOQDs, but only showed a little PL blue-shift of 7 nm when compared to the GOQDs [24]. Obviously, the F-GOQDs-3 exhibit ∼ one order of magnitude larger PL blue-shift level than that of rGOQDs compared to the GOQDs. In other words, the photochemical functionalizing with fluorine plays more important role than only photochemical reduction in the blue-shift of GOQDs.

In principle, the band gap of GQDs is highly influenced by their size due to the quantum confinement effect [39]. The band gap of the GQDs increases as the size of the GQDs decreases [40,41]. For example, Peng et al. [42] reported that the PL emission of the GQDs was blue-shifted when their size decreased due to the increased band gap of the GQDs. However, our experimental results demonstrate that the size of the F-GOQDs does not significantly alter compared to that of the GOQDs (Fig. 2a–d). Thus, we suggest that the PL blue-shift of the F-GOQDs is not primarily influenced by their size due to the quantum confinement effect.

From the experimental results and analysis, the PL mechanism for the F-GOQDs is complex and remains to be explored. A widely-accepted luminescence mechanism is still lacked. To study the underlying physical mechanism, first-principles calculations are performed using the VASP code, which is based on DFT. The PL is the inverse process of optical absorption, which can be described by the imaginary part of the dielectric function. The results of the chemically modified GQD which is constructed in a (5 × 5) supercell are shown in Fig. 5 . The calculated imaginary part of the dielectric function (ε 2) is shown in Fig. 5a, as a function of photon energy for the GOQD and fluorinated graphene oxide quantum dot (F-GOQD). It is found that the partial substitution of OH with F leads to clear blue-shifts of the photo absorption (equivalently, photo emission) peaks. For the peak at ∼2.9 eV, the shoulder position is blue-shifted from ∼2.66 eV (wavelength: λ ∼ 467.9 nm) to ∼ 2.76 eV (λ ∼ 450.5 nm). Blue-shift is also observed at the peak located at ∼4 eV, from 3.97 eV (λ ∼ 313.2 nm) to 4.02 eV (λ ∼ 309.6 nm). Such phenomenon can be explained as follows: The OH group and F are isoelectronic species, while the electronegativity of F is larger than OH, which implies that the electrons of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) will be situated at deeper energy levels. Indeed, our calculations show that, the HOMO of F-GOQD is lowered by ∼0.15 eV with comparison to the GOQD.

By decorating O atoms (instead of OH) at the edge of GQD, we can simulate the situation of GOQD where CO double bonds present (Fig. 5b). The partial substitution of O with F, i.e., one of the CO double bonds is replaced by the CF bond, significant blue-shifts in the optical spectrum are observed. For instance, the peaks locating at ∼2.13 eV (λ ∼ 583.6 nm), 2.66 eV (λ ∼ 467.3 nm), 3.43 eV (λ ∼ 362.4 nm) and 4.17 eV (λ ∼ 298.1 nm) of the optical spectrum of GOQD, are blue-shifted to ∼ 2.31 eV (λ ∼ 538.2 nm), 2.69 eV (λ ∼ 462.1 nm), 3.70 eV (λ ∼ 336.0 nm), and 4.37 eV (λ ∼ 284.5 nm), respectively. Such blue-shifts can be similarly explained by the larger electronegativity of F, owing to which the valence electrons of the corresponding orbitals of F-GOQD locate at deeper energy levels than in GOQD.

Fig. 6 shows the optical absorption spectra of the GOQD and F-GOQD, which are constructed in a (9 × 9) supercell of graphene. The GOQD is simulated by decorating six OH groups at the edge of the GQD, with two of the OH dissociated into O and H, forming two CO and CH bonds. When half of the OH (and O) groups are substituted by F to get F-GOQD, one sees again blue-shifts in the optical spectrum. For example, the peaks locating at ∼2.11 eV (λ ∼ 589.2 nm), 3.57 eV (λ ∼ 348.2 nm), are respectively blue-shifted to ∼ 2.16 eV (λ ∼ 575.5 nm), 3.62 eV (λ ∼ 343.4 nm). These results indicate the general trend of blue-shift in the PL of GOQD upon fluorination.

In conclusion, this work reports a novel and effective photochemical method to prepare F-GOQDs with high contents of fluorine. The obtained F-GOQDs possess relatively uniform size, exhibit tunable luminescence and enjoy good solubility and stability in water. Moreover, the F-GOQDs show a maximal PL blue-shift of ca. 77 nm compared to that of GOQDs. Both experimental and computational analysis reveal that the oxygenic groups have a significant positive influence on the fluorination reaction in GOQDs. Among these oxygenic groups, the hydroxyls and carbonyls can be easily replaced by fluorine atom, which can tune the band gap of the GOQDs, and consequently can lead to the PL blue-shift of F-GOQDs. Our research results suggest a simple route for the PL tuning of GOQDs and a possible mechanism for tuning the electronic band through fluorination. These results can be useful for future graphene-based optoelectronic applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 11664003, 11474285), the Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No.2015GXNSFAA139015), and the Scientific Research and Technology Development Program of Guilin (No.2016012002), and the Key Project of Guangxi Normal University (No. 2014ZD003), P. R. China. We gratefully acknowledge the crew of Center for Computational Materials Science of the Institute for Materials Research, Tohoku University for their continuous support of the SR16000 supercomputing facilities. We thank the staff of the Hefei Branch of Supercomputing Center of Chinese Academy of Sciences for their support of supercomputing resources. Fuhua Gao and Fuchi Liu contributed equally to this study.
